name: Kit: Burglar
raw_text:
Burglar 
Description: The consummate Bur- 
ar is an expert at breaking and enter- 
the most difficult buildings, 
ypassing walls, locks, traps and 
uardians, grabbing the best loot, and 
aping unnoticed as stealthily as he 
The cat Burglar requires a minimum 
Strength of 10 and Dexterity of 13. 
Role: In many ways, the cat Burglar 
is the stereotypical professional thief. 
He probably uses more of the tradition- 
al thief skills, and more frequently, 
than any other kit. 
Even within the ranks of Burglars, 
thieves often specialize even further. 
Some specialize by skills. A "box-man," 
for instance, is an expert at opening 
locks, especially safes and well- 
protected chests. A cat Burglar or 
second-story thief specializes in climb- 
ing walls (which can be a remarkably 
effective protection, especially if 
ground-level entrances have people 
around them). Teams of Burglars who 
specialize by skill often find the most 
Other Burglars specialize by target. 
Jewel thieves in particular are the elite 
among Burglars; the protection found 
around the objects of their attention de- 
mands that their skills and cleverness 
be honed to perfection. 
Burglars of any background may be 
found. Even thrillseekers of the privi- 
leged classes may take up jewel Bur- 
glary as a challenging, profitable, and 
ewiting pastime. 
4lmost all successful Burglars haw 
ne sort of guild affiliation. In ordel 
get rid of the loot they take, they 01 
irse need a fence (especially if theil 
)re is distinctive-e.g., 
fabulou! 
ns, valuable artwork). Guilds pro 
,ide the Burglar with innumerable 
lenefits: fencing of even the most dis- 
inctive items, connections with poten- 
ial "business" partners, access to 
pecialized equipment, and, not least of 
dl, protection. A guild can arrange the 
ix (to free an imprisoned Burglar), and 
wovide deterrence, protecting its Bur- 
:lars from other guilds and powerful 
nminals-people who don't take kind- 
y to being robbed themselves, and are 
nore likely to make hasty decisions 
ibout a suspect character's guilt or in- 
iocence. 
Secondary Skills: Any. 
Weapon Proficiencies: Better Bur- 
gars do not bring weapons with them 
m a job; it only means more serious 
xnalties if they are caught-either 
le- 
91 penalties, or more immediate ones 
ike a jumpy victim panicking and at- 
acking them. On some jobs, however 
:e.g., stealing from dangerous crimi- 
ials) a Burglar is wise to have means of 
self-defense. Small, quiet, concealable 
weapons are naturally favored, though 
a Burglar may choose proficiency in 
any weapon among those normally 
permitted to thieves. 
Nonweapon Proficiencies: Required: 
Alertness, Looting. Recommended: 
Begging, 
Information Gathering, 
Jumping, Observation, Rope Use. 
Tightrope Walking, Tumbling. 
Skill Progression: The vital skills of a 
Burglar are open locks, findhemove 
traps, move silently, hide in shadows. de- 
tect noise and climb walls. As mentioned 
More, a Burglar may concentrate partic- 
ularly on one of these, but he would 
probably then want to be as e d y  exd- 
lent as possible in the othas. 
Equipment: Burglars love to use spc 
cialized hardware to increase their 
chances of success. For a thorough ex- 
amination of some specialty items 
available, and their effects on thief 
skills, consult the equipment chapter 
later in this book. 
Special Benefits: None. 
Special Hindrances: None. 
Races: Members of any race may be 
Burglars, and it is a favorite kit. Non- 
human thieves often specialize in areas 
Thief Kits 
iat offer excellent racial bonuses. F, 
istance, dwarves may specialize in 
xkpicking and trap detection. And 
Ives may specialize in reconnaissance 
hey sneak around and neport on the 
resence and nature of obstacles). 
The Specialist Burglar 
A broad, general range of skills is of- 
en what adventurers choose, but for 
he urban Burglar, specialization is the 
ray to go. There are a number of rea- 
o m  for this. 
A specialized thief is simply more 
narketable. People in the underworld 
vant sommne excellent for a job. That 
nay mean a high-level generalized 
hid, one who has been in the business 
ong enough to be good at everything. 
3ut it's not easy for a thief to reach that 
evel. Therefore, by conaentrating on 
me skill, a relatively low-level thief 
nay compete with a thieF many levels 
ligher for jobs of a certain type. 
Suppose, for instance, we have a 
'box-man"-actually 
a woman- 
lamed Annelise. By concentrating as 
nany points as possible in her open 
ocks skill, she can have a score of 85 % 
at only 4th level (this does not include 
modifiers for race, Dexterity, armor or 
kit), Since she can put no more than 
half of what she earns at each level into 
any one skill, she distributes her re- 
maining points more or less evenly 
among the other skills. She would 
probably neglect pick pockets and read 
languages, however, since they usually 
are not useful to a Burglar. 
Compare this to a "generalist" thief, 
which adventurers tend to be: On Table 
19 of the DUNGEON MASTER Guide 
(Thief Average Ability Table). you can 
see that Annelise's level of lockpicking 
skill would not be attained before 14th 
level. 
Now imagine that a mob of jewel 
thieves is preparing for a job. They 
have diverse skills-except 
that they 
are lousy at lock-picking. They need to 
bring a box-man into their mob. Who 
would they choose? Well, first off - 
14th-level thief is pretty bloody rari 
I3

rhief Kits 
And even if one were available and 
willing to work with less-experienced 
thieves, he would probably demand a 
larger share of the take. Otherwise it 
would not be worth his time: He has 
uniformly good skills, and could prob- 
ably commit this robbery on his own. 
A job with which he would need assist- 
ance is probably well out of the range 
of these thieves. 
Annelise, then, is a pretty attractive 
option. She might be able to climb little 
better than a fish, but that's the cat Bur- 
glar's expertise; after he's mounted the 
building, he can lower a rope for the 
less sure-footed. By offering Annelise a 
reasonable share of the loot, the other 
Burglars are almost assured that their 
difficult lock will be opened.
